whatIs.title: What is this and How to use
whatIs.intro: A browser-based calculator for the characteristic impedance Z₀ of a microstrip line — or for the trace width that hits a target impedance.
whatIs.accuracy: Online calculators that rely on closed-form approximations carry subtle errors. This tool runs a 2D finite-element method for higher accuracy.
whatIs.usage: Enter the substrate parameters first. Supply a trace width and Z₀ is computed automatically; supply a target Z₀ and the width is bisection-searched automatically.
whatIs.local: Because the solver runs locally, expect noticeable CPU and memory use during a run. More details <detail>here</detail>.

geometry.ariaLabel: Reference cross-section of a microstrip line

form.title: Parameters
form.inverseLegend: Find trace width from target Z₀
form.adaptiveEnable: Refine where the field changes fastest
form.adaptiveHint: Solves on a coarse mesh, then iteratively refines the highest-error triangles. Turn off for a single fixed-mesh solve.
form.adaptiveAdvancedHint: The loop iterates until the mesh ceiling (~300k triangles) is hit.

adaptive.title: Adaptive convergence history ({{passes}} passes)
adaptive.viewTooltip: Show this pass's mesh + |E| in the heatmap

stopReason.maxPasses: ● Max-passes reached — bump it under Advanced if you need more.
stopReason.triangleCeiling: ● Stopped at the mesh ceiling (~300k tri) — Triangle's internal limit.
stopReason.noRefinementCandidates: ● No refinement candidates left (every η² is zero)

sweep.title: Z₀ frequency response
sweep.empty: The chart appears after a calculation.
sweep.note: All curves include the Kirschning–Jansen dispersion correction.

about.title: About this tool
about.whyFemBody: Most online microstrip calculators rely on Hammerstad–Jensen or Wheeler closed-form approximations. Those formulas drift as conductor thickness grows, the h/W ratio shrinks, or frequency rises. This tool runs a true 2-D quasi-static finite-element method (FEM) directly in the browser, giving rigorous Z₀ values that hold up where the closed-form expressions break down.
about.scope1: Single-ended microstrip only (no differential / CPW / stripline)
about.scope2: Quasi-static, lossless (no tan δ, skin effect, or dispersion yet)

details.title: How this calculator works
details.intro: Background and inner workings of the microstrip-line calculator.

toc.sec2: 2. How this calculator solves the problem

sec1.title: 1. Why the conventional approximations exist
sec1.wheelerBody: Wheeler derived a pair of closed-form expressions for the characteristic impedance of a thin strip on a single dielectric sheet over a ground plane by conformal mapping<cite1></cite1>. The formulas split into a narrow-strip branch (W/h ≤ 1) and a wide-strip branch (W/h > 1); for the wide branch — the regime almost all PCB traces live in — the result is the pair below. Wheeler's expressions are quoted as accurate to about 1 % in ε_eff and a few percent in Z₀, and they remain the historical reference popularised through Pozar's textbook<cite4></cite4>.
sec1.wheelerCaption: Wheeler's wide-strip pair, with h the substrate thickness, W the trace width, and ε_r the substrate's relative permittivity. The conductor is assumed infinitely thin.
sec1.hjBody: Hammerstad and Jensen refined Wheeler's expressions by fitting auxiliary correction factors a(u) and b(ε_r) against numerical and measured data<cite2></cite2>. The result is a single formula that removes the narrow/wide regime split and pushes the quoted accuracy to roughly 0.03 % in Z₀ and 0.01 % in ε_eff — provided the design sits inside the fitting envelope: thin conductors, single homogeneous substrate, quasi-TEM operation, and a moderate W/h ratio.
sec1.hjCaption: Hammerstad–Jensen ε_eff. The auxiliary factors a(u), b(ε_r) are smooth rational expressions tabulated in the original paper; together they absorb the discrepancy with measured / conformal-mapping reference data inside the fitting envelope.
sec1.commonBody: The published accuracy figures hold only inside the regime each formula was fitted against. Outside it — appreciable conductor thickness, very narrow or very wide traces, multilayer dielectrics, anisotropy, high ε_r, or millimetre-wave frequencies — the result is silent drift: the formula returns a clean number with no error indicator<cite1></cite1><cite2></cite2>. In board design, frequency dispersion is the failure mode that actually bites in practice, and Kirschning and Jansen later derived a separate ε_eff(f) correction that this calculator applies as a post-process on top of the static Z₀<cite3></cite3>.

sec2.title: 2. How this calculator solves the problem
sec2.intro: Rather than evaluating a pre-fitted formula, the calculator runs a 2-D finite-element solve directly on the cross-section of the entire design space (microstrip + substrate + air pad). The unknown is the electric potential φ(x, y); under quasi-TEM conditions it satisfies the inhomogeneous Laplace equation:
sec2.bvpCaption: The conductor surface is held at φ = 1 V, the ground plane and the outer truncation box are held at φ = 0, and the substrate–air interface is handled automatically by the weak form.
sec2.weakBody: Multiplying by a test function v that vanishes on the Dirichlet boundaries and integrating by parts gives the weak form solved numerically<cite4></cite4>:
sec2.energyBody: The domain is tessellated into linear triangles, the integral collapses into a sparse symmetric stiffness matrix K, and the resulting linear system is solved with a Jacobi-preconditioned conjugate gradient method. Capacitance per unit length follows from the stored energy of the converged potential; running the same FEM a second time with ε_r ≡ 1 yields the vacuum capacitance C₀, which decouples the inductance L = μ₀ε₀/C₀ from the dielectric:
sec2.energyCaption: Here K is the assembled stiffness matrix, c is the speed of light in vacuum, and ε_eff is the standard effective relative permittivity.
sec2.whyBetter: Because the geometry enters the FEM through the mesh rather than through a fixed analytical form, there is no fitting envelope. As long as the mesh resolves the conductor edges, the substrate, and the air pad above, the answer is governed by discretisation error — which we control — instead of by how far the design sits from a curve fit.

sec3.title: 3. Mesh and computational shortcuts
sec3.body: Triangle (the Delaunay mesher by Shewchuk<cite6></cite6>, compiled to WebAssembly) lays down a quality-bounded triangulation with extra refinement near the conductor edges where the field-gradient error indicator <eta></eta> is largest. An adaptive loop solves on a coarse mesh, refines the triangles with the highest η², and stops once ΔZ₀ falls below a user-set tolerance. Both FEM passes (with-dielectric and vacuum) reuse the same mesh, and the entire solve runs in a Web Worker so the user interface stays responsive. The Kirschning–Jansen<cite3></cite3> dispersion correction is applied as a closed-form post-process: the FEM itself is purely quasi-static.

sec4.title: 4. Comparison against closed-form formulas
sec4.intro: To make the difference concrete, here are a few parameter sweeps showing how far Hammerstad–Jensen and Wheeler drift apart for identical geometries, and where the FEM answer lands. Markers are FEM values computed live by this tool, the solid line is HJ, the dashed line is Wheeler. The FEM markers are evaluated one by one in a Web Worker, so they fill in progressively after you open the page.
sec4.plot1Title: Fig. 1: Sweep over trace width W (FR-4, h = 1.6 mm, t = 35 µm)
sec4.plot1Caption: Z₀ versus W on a typical FR-4 stack-up over 0.5–4.0 mm. In the narrow regime (W/h < 1) Hammerstad–Jensen and Wheeler can disagree by several percent.
sec4.plot2Title: Fig. 2: Sweep over substrate ε_r (W = 1 mm, h = 0.5 mm, t = 35 µm)
sec4.plot2Caption: Z₀ across a range of representative laminates (FR-4 ε_r ≈ 4.4, Rogers RO4350B ≈ 3.66, RT/duroid 5880 ≈ 2.2, Alumina ≈ 9.8). The spread between the two closed-form expressions widens at higher ε_r.
sec4.plot3Title: Fig. 3: Sweep over conductor thickness t (FR-4, W = 1 mm, h = 0.5 mm)
sec4.plot3Caption: Wheeler assumes an infinitely thin conductor and is therefore flat in t, while Hammerstad–Jensen applies a thickness correction. Even at a realistic copper thickness of 35 µm the two answers can differ by a few percent.

sec5.title: 5. Detailed usage guide
sec5.intro: The toggle at the top right switches between Simple and Advanced modes. Day-to-day use is best served by Simple; switch to Advanced when you want to tune the mesh or inspect the frequency response.
sec5.simpleBody: Enter the substrate parameters (trace width W, substrate height h, conductor thickness t, relative permittivity ε_r, frequency f) and press “Calculate.” Z₀, ε_eff and the related quantities appear in the Result pane. To go the other way, fill in “Z₀ → W (Search)” with a target Z₀ and let the bisection back-solve for W. Setting f to a non-zero value applies the Kirschning–Jansen dispersion correction, so the displayed Z₀ and the Search target both refer to the frequency-dependent Z₀(f) rather than the static value.
sec5.advancedBody: Advanced exposes everything Simple offers plus (a) a tolerance setting for Search (Tolerance ± [%]), (b) the Adaptive mesh controls — enable / disable plus the ΔZ₀ convergence threshold, and (c) the Z₀(f) SweepChart panel. Adaptive mesh solves once on a coarse triangulation and then iteratively refines the highest-error triangles, recording the convergence history pass by pass. The SweepChart renders Z₀(f) for the most recent geometry in Plotly and overlays the Hammerstad–Jensen and Wheeler reference curves for direct comparison.
sec5.tipsBody:
• Start in Simple to fix the geometry and sanity-check the headline Z₀.
• Switch to Advanced when you need to see how Z₀ behaves over a band, or when adaptive refinement is warranted.
• The FEM benefit is largest when the design sits far from the fitting envelope of the closed-form formulas (very narrow / wide traces, non-negligible conductor thickness, high ε_r) — see §1.3.
• The mm / mil unit toggle lives in the header.
